title: Stopping distance — thinking and braking
examTip: Stopping distance = thinking distance + braking distance. Speed affects both, but the relationship is different: thinking distance is proportional to speed (double speed → double thinking distance); braking distance has a quadratic relationship (double speed → four times the braking distance). Always explain factors in terms of physics, not just 'it is harder to stop'.
introTips: Stopping distance is one of the most practically important topics in GCSE Physics — it directly explains road safety rules like speed limits and drink-driving laws.
Thinking distance depends on reaction time and speed — it is the distance the car travels while the driver processes the situation and presses the brake.
Braking distance depends on speed (quadratically) and the grip between tyres and road.
Braking force transfers the car's kinetic energy to thermal energy in the brakes — very heavy braking can cause brakes to overheat.
The quadratic relationship between speed and braking distance is why motorway speed limits save so many lives.
intro: Every road safety rule — speed limits, drink-drive limits, the two-second rule — is rooted in the physics of stopping distance. When something goes wrong ahead of you on a motorway, your car does not stop the moment your brain registers danger. Your brain takes time to process and react; then your brakes take time to bring the car to a halt. Understanding the physics behind each phase tells you why a tired driver at 70 mph needs far more than twice the road space of a fresh driver at 35 mph. It is also why worn tyres and icy roads are so dangerous even at modest speeds.

keyPoints.heading: Stopping distance = thinking distance + braking distance
keyPoints.content: Stopping distance is the total distance a vehicle travels from when the driver first notices a hazard to when the vehicle comes to a complete stop. It has two distinct phases: Thinking distance: the distance travelled while the driver reacts — from seeing the hazard to pressing the brake. Thinking distance = speed × reaction time. Braking distance: the distance travelled once the brakes are applied, while the car decelerates to a stop. Stopping distance = thinking distance + braking distance. Example values (AQA data): at 30 mph, thinking distance ≈ 9 m, braking distance ≈ 14 m, stopping distance ≈ 23 m. At 70 mph, thinking distance ≈ 21 m, braking distance ≈ 75 m, stopping distance ≈ 96 m — roughly four car-lengths to 24 car-lengths.
writeBullets: Stopping distance = thinking distance + braking distance
Thinking distance: distance travelled while driver reacts (before brakes applied)
Braking distance: distance travelled once brakes are applied until vehicle stops
Thinking distance = speed × reaction time
Both thinking and braking distance increase with speed

keyTerms.def: Total distance from first noticing a hazard to coming to a complete stop; equals thinking distance + braking distance
Distance travelled during the driver's reaction time before brakes are applied; = speed × reaction time
Distance travelled while the brakes are applied and the vehicle decelerates to rest

keyPoints.memTip: Stop = Think + Brake. Both increase with speed. Thinking distance ∝ speed; braking distance ∝ speed².
keyPoints.tutor_note: Mabel should be able to identify the two phases from a diagram showing a car journey from hazard to stop, and calculate thinking distance if given speed and reaction time.

keyPoints.heading: Factors affecting reaction time and thinking distance
keyPoints.content: Reaction time is the time between seeing a hazard and pressing the brake — typically 0.2–0.9 seconds for an alert, sober driver. Factors that increase reaction time (making thinking distance longer): Alcohol — slows brain processing significantly. Drugs (prescription or recreational) — many impair reaction speed. Tiredness — fatigue slows brain responses; falling asleep at the wheel is possible. Distractions — using a mobile phone, adjusting a stereo, or talking to passengers. Age — reaction times tend to slow with age. Higher speed also increases thinking distance directly: if reaction time is fixed at 0.5 s, a car travelling at 20 m/s covers 10 m while a car at 40 m/s covers 20 m before the brakes are even applied. This is why increasing speed has such a dramatic effect on stopping distance.
writeBullets: Typical reaction time: 0.2–0.9 seconds (alert, sober driver)
Alcohol: slows brain processing → longer reaction time → greater thinking distance
Drugs: many impair reaction speed → longer thinking distance
Tiredness: fatigue slows brain response; severe tiredness can cause microsleep
Distractions (phone, music, conversation): attention split → slower hazard detection

keyTerms.def: The time between detecting a hazard and pressing the brake — typically 0.2–0.9 seconds for an alert driver

keyPoints.memTip: ATDS: Alcohol, Tiredness, Drugs, Distractions — all increase reaction time and therefore thinking distance.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Exam questions often ask 'why does alcohol increase stopping distance?' — answer must specifically say it increases reaction time and therefore thinking distance, not just 'slows you down'.

keyPoints.heading: Factors affecting braking distance
keyPoints.content: Braking distance is affected by several factors: Speed (most important): braking distance is proportional to speed squared — double the speed → four times the braking distance (because kinetic energy = ½mv², and braking distance is proportional to KE). At 30 mph, braking distance ≈ 14 m; at 60 mph ≈ 55 m; at 70 mph ≈ 75 m. Road surface condition: wet or icy roads reduce friction between tyres and road → less braking force available → longer braking distance. Ice can reduce friction to nearly zero. Tyre condition: worn tyres have less tread → less grip → reduced braking force → longer braking distance. UK legal minimum tyre tread depth: 1.6 mm. Brake condition: worn or overheated brakes apply less force. Brake fade (overheated brakes lose effectiveness) can occur on long downhill sections.
writeBullets: Speed: braking distance ∝ v² — double speed → four times the braking distance
Wet or icy roads: reduced friction between tyres and road → less braking force → longer braking distance
Worn tyres: less tread → reduced grip → less friction → longer braking distance
Worn or overheated brakes: reduced braking force → longer braking distance
All factors reduce the friction/force available — the car needs more distance to lose the same kinetic energy

keyTerms.def: The force applied by the brakes to decelerate the vehicle; transferred from kinetic energy to thermal energy in the brakes
The force between tyre and road surface that allows braking — reduced by ice, water, or worn tyres

keyPoints.memTip: Braking distance ∝ v². Double speed → 4× distance. Anything that reduces friction increases braking distance.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The quadratic relationship between speed and braking distance catches students out — they often say 'double the distance' for double the speed. It is quadruple.

keyPoints.heading: Energy analysis of braking
keyPoints.content: When the brakes are applied, the car decelerates — the kinetic energy of the car is transferred to thermal energy (heat) in the brakes through friction. Kinetic energy: KE = ½mv². Because KE depends on v², doubling speed quadruples KE and therefore quadruples the braking distance (assuming constant braking force). The brakes, tyres, and road surface all heat up during braking. Very heavy braking (emergency stops, long downhill sections) can cause brakes to overheat, reducing their effectiveness — called brake fade. Large deceleration = large braking force = large rate of KE loss. If the deceleration is very high (emergency braking), the forces on passengers are very large — this is why seatbelts and crumple zones are essential. Newton's Second Law: F = ma → braking force = mass × deceleration.
writeBullets: Braking transfers kinetic energy to thermal energy in the brakes
KE = ½mv² — doubling speed quadruples KE and therefore braking distance (at constant braking force)
Brakes, tyres and road all heat up during braking
Brake fade: overheated brakes lose effectiveness — dangerous on long descents
F = ma: larger braking force → larger deceleration → shorter stopping distance

keyTerms.def: Energy of a moving object; KE = ½mv²; transferred to thermal energy in brakes during stopping
Reduction in braking effectiveness caused by overheated brakes — a hazard during prolonged or repeated heavy braking

keyPoints.memTip: KE = ½mv². Speed doubles → KE quadruples → braking distance quadruples. Always energy that must be transferred to heat.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Link back to the work-energy principle: work done by brakes = KE lost. Braking distance = KE ÷ braking force. This gives the quadratic relationship.

tables.title: Stopping distances at different speeds (AQA guidance values)

0.question: A driver has a reaction time of 0.6 s and is travelling at 20 m/s. What is the thinking distance?
0.correctFeedback: Correct! Thinking distance = speed × reaction time = 20 × 0.6 = 12 m.
0.wrongFeedback: Thinking distance = speed × reaction time = 20 m/s × 0.6 s = 12 m. The car travels 12 m before the brakes are even applied.

1.question: A car's braking distance at 20 m/s is 25 m. What would the braking distance be at 40 m/s (on the same road)?
1.correctFeedback: Correct! Braking distance ∝ v². Speed doubles → distance quadruples: 25 × 4 = 100 m.
1.wrongFeedback: Braking distance is proportional to speed squared. Double the speed → (2)² = 4 times the braking distance: 25 × 4 = 100 m. Not 50 m — it is quadratic, not linear.

3.question: Which energy transfer occurs in the brakes as a car stops?
options: Kinetic energy transferred to thermal energy (heat) in the brakes
Potential energy transferred to kinetic energy in the brakes
Thermal energy transferred to kinetic energy in the brakes
Electrical energy transferred to kinetic energy in the brakes
3.correctFeedback: Correct! The brakes apply a friction force that slows the car — kinetic energy is transferred to thermal energy in the brakes, tyres and road surface.
3.wrongFeedback: Braking converts kinetic energy to thermal energy via friction. That is why brakes get hot — they are absorbing the car's kinetic energy as heat.

commonMistakes: Saying braking distance doubles when speed doubles — it quadruples (because KE ∝ v², braking distance ∝ v² at constant braking force).
Mixing up thinking distance and braking distance — thinking distance is before the brakes are applied; braking distance is after.
Saying alcohol or tiredness increases stopping distance without specifying which component — it increases thinking distance (by increasing reaction time), not braking distance.
Forgetting that road and tyre conditions affect braking distance, not thinking distance — wet roads do not affect reaction time.
Not giving the energy analysis when asked why braking distance increases with speed — always link it to KE = ½mv² being proportional to v².
examTips: Thinking distance = speed × reaction time — a one-mark calculation that is easy to set up if you know this formula.
For quadratic relationship questions: state 'braking distance is proportional to speed squared' before giving the calculation — this earns the explanation mark.
Factors affecting thinking distance vs braking distance — keep them separate: alcohol/tiredness/distractions affect thinking; road/tyre/brake condition affects braking.
Energy questions: link braking to KE = ½mv² → transferred to thermal energy in brakes. This explains why faster cars need more braking distance.
revisionCardBullets: Stopping distance = thinking distance + braking distance
Reaction time increased by: alcohol, drugs, tiredness, distractions
Braking distance ∝ v² — double speed → four times the braking distance
Braking distance increased by: wet/icy road, worn tyres, worn/hot brakes
At 30 mph: ~9 m thinking + ~14 m braking = ~23 m total
At 70 mph: ~21 m thinking + ~75 m braking = ~96 m total
Braking transfers kinetic energy to thermal energy in brakes
KE = ½mv² — doubling speed quadruples KE and braking distance
Brake fade: overheated brakes lose effectiveness
